---
title: "11 · Combinatorics — Counting"
subtitle: "How many ways? Three rules underlie all of counting"
lesson-id: "t3.combinatorics-counting"
tier: 3
prerequisites: ["t1.divide-fair-share"]
unlocks: ["t3.binomial-theorem", "t3.basic-probability"]
applied-domains: ["encryption", "ai-ml", "research", "databases"]
estimated-minutes: 30
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Comfortable with multiplication and division ([T1
   L05](../../tier-1-foundations/05-multiply-as-area/index.qmd) and
   [T1 L06](../../tier-1-foundations/06-divide-fair-share/index.qmd))?
2. Have you played card games — and ever wondered "how many possible
   hands?"
3. Comfortable with the factorial: $5! = 5 \cdot 4 \cdot 3 \cdot 2 \cdot 1
   = 120$?
:::

## Touch

Pick $n$ and $r$. The widget shows three counts:

- **arrangements ($n^r$)** — choices with repetition, order matters
- **permutations $P(n,r) = n!/(n-r)!$** — without repetition, order matters
- **combinations $C(n,r) = n!/(r!(n-r)!)$** — without repetition, order doesn't matter

```{ojs}
viewof nVal = Inputs.range([1, 12], {value: 5, step: 1, label: "n (items available):"})
viewof rVal = Inputs.range([0, 12], {value: 3, step: 1, label: "r (items chosen):"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const factorial = n => n <= 1 ? 1 : n * factorial(n - 1);
  const safeR = Math.min(rVal, nVal);
  const arr = Math.pow(nVal, rVal);
  const perm = safeR > nVal ? 0 : factorial(nVal) / factorial(nVal - safeR);
  const comb = safeR > nVal ? 0 : perm / factorial(safeR);
  return html`<div style="text-align:center; margin: 1rem 0;">
    <table style="margin: 0 auto; border-collapse:collapse; font-family:monospace; font-size:1.05rem;">
      <tr style="background:#f5f5f5;"><th style="padding:6px 14px;">Count</th><th style="padding:6px 14px;">Formula</th><th style="padding:6px 14px;">Value</th></tr>
      <tr style="border-top:1px solid #ccc;"><td style="padding:4px 14px;color:#1565c0;">Arrangements (with repetition)</td><td style="padding:4px 14px;">${nVal}<sup>${rVal}</sup></td><td style="padding:4px 14px;font-weight:bold;">${arr.toLocaleString()}</td></tr>
      <tr style="border-top:1px solid #ccc;"><td style="padding:4px 14px;color:#c2185b;">Permutations P(${nVal},${rVal})</td><td style="padding:4px 14px;">${nVal}!/(${nVal}−${safeR})!</td><td style="padding:4px 14px;font-weight:bold;">${perm.toLocaleString()}</td></tr>
      <tr style="border-top:1px solid #ccc;"><td style="padding:4px 14px;color:#2e7d32;">Combinations C(${nVal},${rVal})</td><td style="padding:4px 14px;">${nVal}!/(${safeR}!(${nVal}−${safeR})!)</td><td style="padding:4px 14px;font-weight:bold;">${comb.toLocaleString()}</td></tr>
    </table>
  </div>`;
}
```

The three central counts. Order? Repetition? — answer those questions
and the right formula picks itself.

## Wonder

1. From 4 letters, how many 3-letter "words" if letters can repeat?
   Cannot repeat?
2. Why are the *combinations* fewer than the *permutations*?
3. From 50 students, how many ways to pick a committee of 5?

## Discover

### Problem 1

A pizza shop offers **3 sizes**, **5 crust types**, and **8 toppings**
(pick one). How many distinct pizzas?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
**Multiplication principle:** if step 1 has $a$ options, step 2 has
$b$, ..., total is $a \cdot b \cdot \ldots$
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$3 \cdot 5 \cdot 8 = 120$ pizzas. The **multiplication principle** is
the foundation of all counting.
</details>

### Problem 2

How many ways to arrange 5 books on a shelf?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
First slot: 5 choices. Second: 4. Third: 3. Etc.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$5 \cdot 4 \cdot 3 \cdot 2 \cdot 1 = 5! = 120$ arrangements.

The number of arrangements of $n$ distinct objects is **$n!$**
(*"$n$ factorial"*). For $n = 10$, that's already 3.6 million.
</details>

### Problem 3

A 3-digit PIN can use digits 0–9, repetition allowed. How many?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$10 \cdot 10 \cdot 10 = 10^3 = 1000$. With repetition allowed:
$n^r$ arrangements (here $n = 10, r = 3$).
</details>

### Problem 4

How many 3-digit codes using 0–9 with **no repetition**?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$10 \cdot 9 \cdot 8 = 720$.

This is the **permutation** count: $P(n, r) = \dfrac{n!}{(n-r)!}$.
$P(10, 3) = 10!/7! = 720$.
</details>

### Problem 5

A committee of **3 people** is chosen from a class of **10**. How
many committees? (Order doesn't matter — Anil-Bina-Cara is the same
committee as Cara-Anil-Bina.)

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Permutations $P(10, 3) = 720$ over-count, because every committee is
counted $3! = 6$ times (once per ordering). So divide.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\dfrac{P(10, 3)}{3!} = \dfrac{720}{6} = 120$.

This is the **combination** count: $C(n, r) = \binom{n}{r} =
\dfrac{n!}{r!(n - r)!}$.

$C(10, 3) = 120$. **120 possible committees.**

The "n choose r" notation $\binom{n}{r}$ is read aloud as "n choose r"
and is the number of $r$-element *subsets* of an $n$-set.
</details>

::: {.fun-fact}
The Italian polymath **Gerolamo Cardano**, in his *Liber de Ludo
Aleae* ("Book on Games of Chance," written ~1564, published 1663),
was the first European to count outcomes of dice rolls
systematically — opening the door to probability. A century earlier
in China, **Yang Hui** (杨辉, c. 1238–1298) tabulated the triangular
array of binomial coefficients we now call *Pascal's triangle* — over
three hundred years before Pascal. Counting argued itself into
mathematics from the gambling table and the court ledger long before
it became a "branch."
:::

::: {.key-insight}
**Two questions decide every counting problem: does order matter?
can things repeat?** Get those answers right and the formula
$\bigl( n^r,\ P(n,r),\ \binom{n}{r} \bigr)$ picks itself. Get them
wrong and no amount of factorial gymnastics will save you.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

**Multiplication principle:** if a process has $k$ independent steps
with $n_1, n_2, \ldots, n_k$ choices each, the total number of
outcomes is $n_1 \cdot n_2 \cdots n_k$.

**Factorial:** $n! = n(n-1)(n-2) \cdots 1$. By convention $0! = 1$.

**Three counting formulas:**

| Question | Order matters? | Repetition? | Formula |
|---|:-:|:-:|---|
| Arrangements with rep | yes | yes | $n^r$ |
| Permutations | yes | no | $P(n, r) = \dfrac{n!}{(n-r)!}$ |
| Combinations | no | no | $C(n, r) = \binom{n}{r} = \dfrac{n!}{r! (n-r)!}$ |

**Always ask:** *Does order matter? Can items repeat?* The answer
picks the formula.

:::

> *Multiplication principle: choices multiply across independent steps;*
> *Permutations count ordered selections; combinations count unordered;*
> *Order matters? Repetition allowed? — those two questions decide.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> A 5-card poker hand is dealt from a standard 52-card deck. How
> many distinct hands?

Order doesn't matter (the same five cards form the same hand
regardless of dealing order); repetition impossible (each card is
unique).

$\binom{52}{5} = \dfrac{52!}{5! \cdot 47!} = \dfrac{52 \cdot 51 \cdot 50
\cdot 49 \cdot 48}{120} = 2{,}598{,}960$ hands.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** How many ways to arrange the letters of "MATH"?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$4! = 24$ (all letters distinct).
</details>

**W2.** $C(7, 2) = ?$

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\dfrac{7!}{2! \cdot 5!} = \dfrac{42}{2} = 21$.
</details>

**W3.** A 4-digit PIN using digits 0–9, repetition allowed. How many?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$10^4 = 10{,}000$.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** A team of 4 players is chosen from 12 candidates. How many
teams?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\binom{12}{4} = \dfrac{12!}{4! \cdot 8!} = 495$.
</details>

**P2.** How many distinct 5-letter "words" (real or made-up) using the
26 English letters, with **no repeated letter**?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$P(26, 5) = 26 \cdot 25 \cdot 24 \cdot 23 \cdot 22 = 7{,}893{,}600$.
</details>

**P3.** From 8 different books, you choose 3 for a holiday. How many
choices? Then: in how many *orders* can you read them?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
First a combination, then a permutation of those 3.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Choices: $\binom{8}{3} = 56$ sets. Orders for each: $3! = 6$.
**Total order-and-choice combinations: $56 \cdot 6 = 336 = P(8, 3)$**.

This shows: $P(n, r) = C(n, r) \cdot r!$ — permutations =
combinations times orderings of the chosen subset.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** **A password.** Pick **8 characters** from a–z (26), A–Z
(26), 0–9 (10), and 10 special characters — total **72 distinct
characters**, repetition allowed. How many distinct passwords?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$72^8 \approx 7.2 \times 10^{14}$ — about **720 trillion**
passwords. At a billion guesses per second, brute-forcing through all
of them takes ~8 days. (In reality, weak password choices reduce the
*effective* space dramatically; that's why diceware-style passphrases
and password managers are recommended.)

The size of a password space is *the* measure of brute-force
strength. The exponential function makes a small character-set
expansion (62 → 72) much more dramatic than it sounds.
</details>

**S2.** **Lottery odds.** A lottery draws 6 numbers from 49 (no
repetition, order doesn't matter). What's the chance a single ticket
wins the jackpot?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Total combinations: $\binom{49}{6}$. One ticket = 1 favourable.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\binom{49}{6} = \dfrac{49!}{6! \cdot 43!} = 13{,}983{,}816$.

Chance per ticket: $1 / 13{,}983{,}816 \approx 7.15 \times 10^{-8}$
— roughly **1 in 14 million**. To put it in perspective: you are
several times more likely to be struck by lightning in a year (about
1 in a million in many places).

This is why lotteries are sometimes called "a tax on people who can't
do mathematics."
</details>

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"$P(n, r)$ and $C(n, r)$ are interchangeable."** No — order
   matters for $P$, doesn't for $C$. Different problems need different
   formulas.
2. **"$n!$ for very small or very large $n$ is weird."** $0! = 1$ by
   convention (makes formulas work). $50! \approx 3 \times 10^{64}$ —
   astronomically large.
3. **"$\binom{n}{r}$ is just shorthand for some number."** It's a
   genuine count — the number of $r$-element subsets of an $n$-set.
   Has rich combinatorial meaning.
:::

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

1. **The [multiplication principle](/glossary.qmd#multiplication-principle)
   is the engine of counting** — independent steps multiply, full
   stop. Almost every counting formula is just this idea applied
   carefully.
2. **[Permutation](/glossary.qmd#permutation) cares about order;
   [combination](/glossary.qmd#combination) does not.** Divide a
   permutation by $r!$ to collapse the orderings of the chosen $r$.
3. **The [factorial](/glossary.qmd#factorial) explodes ferociously**
   — $52!$ already exceeds the number of atoms in the observable
   universe, which is why "shuffle the deck" gives an arrangement no
   human has ever seen before.

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you state the multiplication principle and apply it to a
   3-step decision?
2. Without scrolling back, what is $\binom{6}{2}$?
3. Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 *Discover* problems and 4 of the 6
   practice problems without peeking past *Hint 1*?

(Answer: 15.)

- **3 yes:** ready for **[12 · Binomial Theorem](../12-binomial-theorem/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo **S1** (password space) — counting argument
  central to security.
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover P5* (the C = P/r! derivation).

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[T1 L06 — Division as Fair Share](../../tier-1-foundations/06-divide-fair-share/index.qmd)** —
  combination formula uses division of factorials.

### What this unlocks

- **[12 · Binomial Theorem](../12-binomial-theorem/index.qmd)** —
  $\binom{n}{r}$ is the coefficient.
- **[13 · Basic Probability](../13-basic-probability/index.qmd)** —
  probability often = (favourable count) / (total count), both
  combinatorial.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Encryption.** Brute-force attack times depend on space sizes
(passwords, key spaces). Modern crypto designs key spaces to be at
least $2^{128}$ — beyond reach of any future computer.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / Machine Learning.** Combinatorial explosion underlies why
exhaustive search is intractable for many ML problems — driving the
need for *gradient*-based and *probabilistic* methods.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Databases.** Counting query optimisation, set sizes, join
cardinalities, group-by aggregates — all combinatorics underneath.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Mathematical research.** Combinatorics is a vast field — graph
theory, design theory, partition theory, algebraic combinatorics —
all built on the basic counting arguments here.
:::
